result:
There's a specific shade of blue that exists only at 5:47 AM, just before the world remembers it needs to hurry. I keep trying to name it—periwinkle, cerulean, steel—but none stick, because this blue is temporary, a secret color reserved for those who choose to witness the day before it becomes performance.

The coffee maker gurgles in the kitchen, a reliable metronome against the silence. Outside, the trees hold their breath. No wind yet, no birdsong demanding attention. Just stillness so complete it feels almost sacred, as if the earth itself is deciding whether today is worth the trouble of continuing. The streetlamps flicker, unsure whether their shift has ended, casting long pools of amber onto empty pavement.

I used to fear mornings like this. The quiet felt accusatory, highlighting every ambition I'd abandoned in yesterday's dusk. But somewhere between thirty and wherever I am now, I learned that silence isn't empty; it's full of answers waiting for the noise to stop. The mind, untethered from notifications and deadlines, wanders into curious territory—old memories resurface, half-formed ideas bloom, and I remember who I am when no one is watching.

A single headlight cuts through the blue, then another. The world exhales. Soon the deliveries will begin, the emails, the relentless scrolling. But for these few minutes, time belongs to itself again. I don't check my phone. I don't plan. I simply sit with the temporary blue, grateful that even in a world that never stops, there are still these small intermissions—brief, borrowed, and entirely mine.
